comments.text: Might it be that the first problem as stated here is missing the assumption that $n+1, \dots, n+k$ must be composite? The DeepMind prover agent proved the first question with a formalization that appears to reflect the statement here (but without the composite assumption), where the inequality in particular fails for k = 26. formal proof here

I don't see anywhere they were assuming all are composite, but there are other problems with this statement - see the older comments by Meza and Tao. I've revisited this and updated the first question to better reflect what was most likely intended - could you check whether the DeepMind counterexample is still valid for this version?

Ah, the "composite" was just coming from another paper by Erdős and Selfridge: "Some Problems on the Prime Factors of Consecutive Integers II") from 1971, but I suppose it is not exactly this question. The counterexample doesn't work anymore with the adapted definitions, will update the statement in Formal Conjectures, thanks!

The first problem Erdös and Selfridge state is not about $\omega (n)$, but a little variation which only counts the prime factors of $n+k$ which are greater than k. Otherwise the sum $\sum_{0 \le i \le k}\omega (n) \gg \log\log(k)k$ from Turán-Kubilius inequality over the set of primes less than $\sqrt{k}$. The case for fixed $k$ would follow from the $k$-tuples conjecture the following way: Let $N$ be sufficiently large in terms of $k$. Then linear forms $L(n;j) = N!n + j$ for $1 \le j \le k+1$ form an admissible $k$-tuple if $N$ is sufficiently large. Then, by the $k$-tuples conjecture, there exists infinitely many $n$ such that $L(n;j)$ is prime for all $1 \le j \le k+1$. This implies that $N!n + j$ is equal to a prime $p_{j}$ times $j$ infinitely often. The sum that Erdös and Selfridge were considering would be equal to $k+ \pi(k)$ (each $N!n + j$ will contribute $1$ if $j-1$ is not prime and $2$ if it is). Note: the condition $j-1$ being prime or not comes from the fact that we've shifted the beginning of the string of consecutive integers to $N!n + 1$.

I'm not sure I understand your first comment - in this question I'm sure that Erdős and Selfridge intended to ask about $\omega$, rather than the variant you describe. (They did discuss other problems involving this variant in that paper.) Indeed, it has to be $\omega$, otherwise the lower bound construction they give doesn't work!

I thik Agustin is correct and Erdos and Selfridge messed up slightly in their statement: every prime $p \leq k$ will in fact divide at least $\lfloor k/p\rfloor$ of the $n,\dots,n+k-1$, so their $\pi(k)$ should really be $\sum_{p \leq k} \lfloor k/p \rfloor$ (which is $\sim k \log\log k$ as Agustin points out). Then the first part of the problem is indeed a variant of the prime tuples conjecture.
